title: Conjecture on Sufficiency of Conditions E, R, T for Generic Tuples
statement_text: When $\Lambda$ is generic, Conditions E, R, and T are necessary and sufficient for an $m \times (n+1)$ matrix to be the multiplicity matrix of some polynomial $P$ with respect to $\Lambda$.
context_text: Theorem 1 of the paper establishes that Conditions E, R, T are necessary and sufficient for a $2 \times (n+1)$ matrix to be a multiplicity matrix. The authors note that for $m > 2$ these three conditions are not sufficient in general (almost all matrices satisfying E, R, C for large $m = n$ are not multiplicity matrices), but conjecture sufficiency is restored when $\Lambda$ is generic. Further discussion is deferred to Section 8.
notes: Stated in running prose in Section 3 without a labelled theorem environment; the authors write 'we conjecture that when $\Lambda$ is generic, Conditions E, R, and T are necessary and sufficient'.

title: Nathanson's Extension Problem [Open Question 4]
statement_text: Given an $m \times (n+1)$ $\{0,1\}$-matrix $D$ (which may or may not itself be dope), does there exist a polynomial $P$ and an $m$-tuple $\Lambda$ of distinct complex numbers such that $D$ appears in $D_P(\Lambda)$ as the submatrix consisting of the first $n+1$ columns?
context_text: Nathanson posed this extension problem in [7, Open Question 4], asking whether any $\{0,1\}$-matrix can be realised as the leading columns of some dope matrix. The paper notes a stronger form is of interest: whether $\Lambda$ can also be fixed beforehand.
paper_contribution: Resolves the problem affirmatively via Theorem 7: for any $m \times (n+1)$ $\{0,1\}$-matrix $D$ and any pre-specified $m$-tuple $\Lambda$ of distinct complex numbers, there exists a polynomial $P$ with $n \leq \deg(P) \leq m(n+2)$ such that $D$ appears in $D_P(\Lambda)$ as the submatrix of the first $n+1$ columns; moreover $P$ may be chosen with coefficients in any subfield containing the entries of $\Lambda$.

title: Nathanson's Open Question 5 (Tightness of Degree Bound)
statement_text: Is the degree bound $\deg(P) \leq m(n+2)$ in the extension problem (Theorem 7) essentially tight?
context_text: Nathanson asked in [7, Open Question 5] about the sharpness of the degree bound required for an extending polynomial. The paper shows that when $D$ is the all-1's matrix the extending polynomial must have degree at least $m(n+1)$, giving a near-matching lower bound.
paper_contribution: Answers affirmatively: for $D$ the all-1's $m \times (n+1)$ matrix, any extending polynomial $P$ must satisfy $\deg(P) \geq m(n+1)$, showing the upper bound $\deg(P) \leq m(n+2)$ of Theorem 7 is nearly tight.
notes: Exact statement of Nathanson's Open Question 5 is not reproduced verbatim in this paper; the statement is reconstructed from the authors' description of their answer. PDF extraction also garbled after the proof of Corollary 2, so Section 8 open problems are unavailable.
